Assignment Week 6-B.Miller

Braniqua Miller

2026-02-12

Homework

Let’s return to the brca dataset. Recall that the response variable y has values “M” and “B” (denoting malignant and benign). Now, we will use more of the predictors.

library(tidyverse)
## ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
## ✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.6
## ✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
## ✔ ggplot2   4.0.1     ✔ tibble    3.3.0
## ✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
## ✔ purrr     1.2.0     
## ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
## ✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
## ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
## ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
library(caret)
## Loading required package: lattice
## 
## Attaching package: 'caret'
## 
## The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':
## 
##     lift
library(dslabs) # for the homework
library('pROC') #This package has a function for computing the area under the ROC curve.
## Type 'citation("pROC")' for a citation.
## 
## Attaching package: 'pROC'
## 
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     cov, smooth, var
data(brca)
brca_data <- data.frame(brca$x) |>
  data.frame(y = brca$y)

set.seed(42)
test_index <- createDataPartition(brca_data$y, p = 0.25, list = FALSE)
test_brca <- brca_data[test_index,]
train_brca <- brca_data[-test_index,]

summary(train_brca)
##   radius_mean      texture_mean   perimeter_mean     area_mean     
##  Min.   : 6.981   Min.   : 9.71   Min.   : 43.79   Min.   : 143.5  
##  1st Qu.:11.750   1st Qu.:16.16   1st Qu.: 75.56   1st Qu.: 426.2  
##  Median :13.320   Median :18.81   Median : 86.04   Median : 546.2  
##  Mean   :14.132   Mean   :19.24   Mean   : 92.01   Mean   : 654.5  
##  3rd Qu.:15.750   3rd Qu.:21.66   3rd Qu.:104.00   3rd Qu.: 780.3  
##  Max.   :27.220   Max.   :39.28   Max.   :182.10   Max.   :2250.0  
##  smoothness_mean   compactness_mean  concavity_mean    concave_pts_mean 
##  Min.   :0.06251   Min.   :0.01938   Min.   :0.00000   Min.   :0.00000  
##  1st Qu.:0.08613   1st Qu.:0.06671   1st Qu.:0.02883   1st Qu.:0.01964  
##  Median :0.09593   Median :0.09625   Median :0.06175   Median :0.03387  
##  Mean   :0.09642   Mean   :0.10511   Mean   :0.08991   Mean   :0.04918  
##  3rd Qu.:0.10577   3rd Qu.:0.13048   3rd Qu.:0.13225   3rd Qu.:0.07466  
##  Max.   :0.16340   Max.   :0.31140   Max.   :0.42680   Max.   :0.20120  
##  symmetry_mean    fractal_dim_mean    radius_se        texture_se    
##  Min.   :0.1060   Min.   :0.04996   Min.   :0.1115   Min.   :0.3602  
##  1st Qu.:0.1619   1st Qu.:0.05812   1st Qu.:0.2279   1st Qu.:0.8370  
##  Median :0.1786   Median :0.06183   Median :0.3307   Median :1.1270  
##  Mean   :0.1812   Mean   :0.06308   Mean   :0.4043   Mean   :1.2114  
##  3rd Qu.:0.1955   3rd Qu.:0.06658   3rd Qu.:0.4832   3rd Qu.:1.4638  
##  Max.   :0.3040   Max.   :0.09744   Max.   :1.5090   Max.   :4.8850  
##   perimeter_se       area_se        smoothness_se      compactness_se    
##  Min.   : 0.757   Min.   :  7.228   Min.   :0.001713   Min.   :0.002252  
##  1st Qu.: 1.604   1st Qu.: 17.688   1st Qu.:0.005213   1st Qu.:0.013213  
##  Median : 2.328   Median : 24.660   Median :0.006251   Median :0.021145  
##  Mean   : 2.860   Mean   : 39.886   Mean   :0.007031   Mean   :0.025981  
##  3rd Qu.: 3.366   3rd Qu.: 45.475   3rd Qu.:0.008097   3rd Qu.:0.033348  
##  Max.   :11.070   Max.   :233.000   Max.   :0.031130   Max.   :0.100600  
##   concavity_se     concave_pts_se      symmetry_se       fractal_dim_se     
##  Min.   :0.00000   Min.   :0.000000   Min.   :0.009539   Min.   :0.0008948  
##  1st Qu.:0.01455   1st Qu.:0.007646   1st Qu.:0.014905   1st Qu.:0.0022673  
##  Median :0.02625   Median :0.010850   Median :0.018710   Median :0.0032175  
##  Mean   :0.03269   Mean   :0.011902   Mean   :0.020577   Mean   :0.0038958  
##  3rd Qu.:0.04296   3rd Qu.:0.014770   3rd Qu.:0.023868   3rd Qu.:0.0046200  
##  Max.   :0.39600   Max.   :0.052790   Max.   :0.061460   Max.   :0.0298400  
##   radius_worst   texture_worst   perimeter_worst    area_worst    
##  Min.   : 7.93   Min.   :12.02   Min.   : 50.41   Min.   : 185.2  
##  1st Qu.:13.05   1st Qu.:21.04   1st Qu.: 84.12   1st Qu.: 515.8  
##  Median :14.91   Median :25.21   Median : 97.45   Median : 679.2  
##  Mean   :16.32   Mean   :25.59   Mean   :107.64   Mean   : 886.9  
##  3rd Qu.:18.94   3rd Qu.:29.41   3rd Qu.:126.20   3rd Qu.:1093.2  
##  Max.   :33.13   Max.   :49.54   Max.   :229.30   Max.   :3432.0  
##  smoothness_worst  compactness_worst concavity_worst  concave_pts_worst
##  Min.   :0.07117   Min.   :0.02729   Min.   :0.0000   Min.   :0.00000  
##  1st Qu.:0.11630   1st Qu.:0.14860   1st Qu.:0.1097   1st Qu.:0.06433  
##  Median :0.13125   Median :0.21810   Median :0.2314   Median :0.09886  
##  Mean   :0.13221   Mean   :0.25597   Mean   :0.2754   Mean   :0.11525  
##  3rd Qu.:0.14607   3rd Qu.:0.34190   3rd Qu.:0.3864   3rd Qu.:0.16510  
##  Max.   :0.21840   Max.   :1.05800   Max.   :1.2520   Max.   :0.29100  
##  symmetry_worst   fractal_dim_worst y      
##  Min.   :0.1566   Min.   :0.05521   B:267  
##  1st Qu.:0.2502   1st Qu.:0.07193   M:159  
##  Median :0.2820   Median :0.08075          
##  Mean   :0.2894   Mean   :0.08455          
##  3rd Qu.:0.3175   3rd Qu.:0.09259          
##  Max.   :0.6638   Max.   :0.20750
SBA_clean <- read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/gmtanner-cord/DATA318/master/Original%20Data/SBA_clean.csv",
                      col_types = cols(State = col_factor(),
            NAICS_2d = col_factor(),
            ApprovalDate = col_date(),
            Term = col_double(),
            RealBacked = col_factor(),
            NoEmp = col_double(),
            NewExist = col_factor(),
            UrbanRural = col_factor(),
            RevLineCr = col_factor(),
            LowDoc = col_factor(),
            DisbursementGross = col_double(),
            Loan_Status = col_factor(),
            Portion_Backed = col_double()
          )) |>
  select(-Portion_Backed) |>
  data.frame()
set.seed(42)
test_index <- createDataPartition(SBA_clean$Loan_Status, p = 0.20, list = FALSE)
test_SBA <- SBA_clean[test_index,]
train_SBA <- SBA_clean[-test_index,]

Problem 1

Choose 4 predictors. Try to choose a variety of variables. Create plots (at least one for each variable) to look at the relationship between each of the variables and the response variable (y). Try density plots (with fill = y) and scatter plots (with color = y). Write a sentence describing the relationship in each plot you create.

brca_data$y <- factor(brca_data$y)

ggplot(brca_data, aes(x = radius_mean, fill = y)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = Inf, y = Inf,label = "The density plot shows tumors with higher radius mean\nvalues have a higher chance of being a malignant class. This\nindicates that a large tumor size correlates with a diagnosis.", hjust = 1, vjust = 1.5, size = 3 )+
  labs(title = "Radius Mean by Diagnosis Density Plot")

ggplot(brca_data, aes(x = radius_mean, y = texture_mean, color  = y)) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0, height = 0.1, alpha =0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = Inf, y = Inf,label = "The scatterplot shows a positive relationship between\nradius and texture mean.Malignant tumors\ngenerally have a higher values for both. ", hjust = 1, vjust = 1.5, size = 2.75 )+
  labs(title = "Radius Mean by Diagnosis Scatter Plot")

ggplot(brca_data, aes(x = texture_mean, fill = y)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) + annotate("text", x = Inf, y = Inf,label = "The density plot shows malignant tumors have a\nslightly higher texture mean values when\ncompared to benign tumors. This shows that\nthere is a moderate relationship with diagnosis(y)", hjust = 1, vjust = 1.3, size = 2.75 )+
  labs(title = "Texture Mean by Diagnosis Density Plot")

ggplot(brca_data, aes(x = texture_mean, y = perimeter_mean, color = y))   +geom_jitter(width = 0, height = 0.1, alpha =0.5) + 
  annotate("text", x = Inf, y = Inf,label = "The plot shows that texture and perimeter\nmean increase together.Malignant\ntumors are more conncentrated\nat high values for both variables", hjust = 1, vjust = 1.4, size = 2.75 )+
  labs(title = "Texture Mean by Diagnosis Scatter Plot")

ggplot(brca_data, aes(x = perimeter_mean, fill = y)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) + 
  annotate("text", x = Inf, y = Inf,label = "The plot shows a shift towards higher perimeter mean values for\nmalignant tumors. This shows a strong correlation to diagnosis", hjust = 1, vjust = 1.4, size = 2.75 )+
  labs(title = "Perimeter Mean by Diagnosis Density Plot")

ggplot(brca_data, aes(x = perimeter_mean, y = area_mean, color = y)) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0, height = 0.1, alpha =0.5) + 
  annotate("text", x = Inf, y = Inf,label = "The plot indicates a strong positive correlation between\nperimeter and area mean with magligant tumors having larger\nperimeters and areas", hjust = 1, vjust = 7, size = 2.75 )+
  labs(title = "Perimeter Mean by Diagnosis Scatter Plot")

ggplot(brca_data, aes(x = area_mean, fill = y)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) + 
  annotate("text", x = Inf, y = Inf,label = "The plot sows that malignant tumors have a larger area\nmean compared to benign tumors, indicating a strong association", hjust = 1, vjust = 1.4, size = 2.75 )+
  labs(title = "Area Mean by Diagnosis Density Plot")

ggplot(brca_data, aes(x = area_mean, y = radius_mean, color = y)) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0, height = 0.1, alpha =0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = Inf, y = Inf,label = "The plot shows a strong positive correlation\nbetween area and radius mean with malignant tumors\nbeing clustered at higher values of both variables", hjust = 1, vjust = 7, size = 2.75 )+
  labs(title = "Area Mean by Diagnosis Scatter Plot")

Problem 2

Create a logistic model to predict the response variable (y) using your 4 predictors. Which of the variables are statistically significant? Create an ROC plot for your model. What is the ROC area under curve?

brca_data$y <- factor(brca_data$y)

model1 <- glm(y ~ radius_mean + texture_mean + perimeter_mean + area_mean, data = brca_data, family = binomial)
## Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred
summary(model1)
## 
## Call:
## glm(formula = y ~ radius_mean + texture_mean + perimeter_mean + 
##     area_mean, family = binomial, data = brca_data)
## 
## Coefficients:
##                Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)     1.77291    6.87011   0.258  0.79636    
## radius_mean    -9.42874    1.63958  -5.751 8.89e-09 ***
## texture_mean    0.23761    0.04603   5.162 2.44e-07 ***
## perimeter_mean  1.15066    0.16436   7.001 2.54e-12 ***
## area_mean       0.03277    0.01182   2.771  0.00558 ** 
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
## 
##     Null deviance: 751.44  on 568  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance: 211.37  on 564  degrees of freedom
## AIC: 221.37
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 8
#All of the variables choosen are statistically significant.

pred_prob <- predict(model1, type = "response")

roc_obj <-roc(response = brca_data$y, predictor = pred_prob, plot = TRUE)
## Setting levels: control = B, case = M
## Setting direction: controls < cases

auc(roc_obj)
## Area under the curve: 0.9779

Problem 3

Suppose the clinicians want a model with at least 90% True Positive Rate (Sensitivity). What is the maximum Specificity (or minimum False Positive Rate) that will guarantee 90% TPR? What probability cutoff does this correspond to?

Create a confusion matrix at this cutoff.

prob_brca <- predict(model1, newdata = test_brca, type = "response")

roc_brca <- roc(test_brca$y, prob_brca)
## Setting levels: control = B, case = M
## Setting direction: controls < cases
cut90 <- coords(roc_brca,
                    x = 0.90,
                    input = "sensitivity",
                    ret = c("threshold", "specificity", "sensitivity"),
                    best.method = "closest")

cutoff <- as.numeric(cut90["threshold"])
spec   <- cut90["specificity"]

cutoff
## [1] NA
spec
##   specificity
## 1   0.8888889
cut90
##   threshold specificity sensitivity
## 1        NA   0.8888889         0.9
pred_class <- ifelse(prob_brca >= cutoff,
                     "B",
                     "M")
pred_class <- factor(pred_class,
                     levels = levels(test_brca$y))

confusionMatrix(pred_class, test_brca$y, positive = "M")
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##           Reference
## Prediction B M
##          B 0 0
##          M 0 0
##                                   
##                Accuracy : NaN     
##                  95% CI : (NA, NA)
##     No Information Rate : NA      
##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : NA      
##                                   
##                   Kappa : NaN     
##                                   
##  Mcnemar's Test P-Value : NA      
##                                   
##             Sensitivity :  NA     
##             Specificity :  NA     
##          Pos Pred Value :  NA     
##          Neg Pred Value :  NA     
##              Prevalence : NaN     
##          Detection Rate : NaN     
##    Detection Prevalence : NaN     
##       Balanced Accuracy :  NA     
##                                   
##        'Positive' Class : M       
##

Problem 4

Interpret the effect of each of your predictor variables on the probability of malignant by comparing 25th and 75th percentile of each variable (while holding all other variables at their median value).

quant_var <-c("radius_mean", "texture_mean", "perimeter_mean", "area_mean")

percentiles <- test_brca %>%
  summarise(across(all_of(quant_var),
                   list(P25 = ~quantile(.x, 0.25),
                        P75 = ~quantile(.x, 0.75),
                        MED = ~median(.x))))
percentiles
##   radius_mean_P25 radius_mean_P75 radius_mean_MED texture_mean_P25
## 1            11.6           15.94           13.45            16.21
##   texture_mean_P75 texture_mean_MED perimeter_mean_P25 perimeter_mean_P75
## 1            21.94            19.07             74.285              104.8
##   perimeter_mean_MED area_mean_P25 area_mean_P75 area_mean_MED
## 1              87.02        412.55         785.6         555.1
#Large increases in radius, perimeter and area have a positive effect on malignancy. Variables with smaller changes have weaker effects

Our baseline model has an ROC area under the curve of 0.7975. We will see if we can improve on that. Answer the following questions using the training and test set.

Problem 1 - Near-Zero Variance Predictors

Check for near-zero variance predictors on the training set. Are there any?

nzv <- nearZeroVar(train_SBA, saveMetrics = T)
nzv
##                   freqRatio percentUnique zeroVar   nzv
## State              1.156231   0.010217810   FALSE FALSE
## NAICS_2d           2.416157   0.028950461   FALSE FALSE
## ApprovalDate       1.736842  11.946322440   FALSE FALSE
## Term               2.039896   0.573900308   FALSE FALSE
## RealBacked         5.968197   0.003405937   FALSE FALSE
## NoEmp              1.163090   0.427445037   FALSE FALSE
## NewExist           2.279486   0.005108905   FALSE FALSE
## UrbanRural         1.190550   0.005108905   FALSE FALSE
## RevLineCr          1.960255   0.005108905   FALSE FALSE
## LowDoc             3.643891   0.005108905   FALSE FALSE
## DisbursementGross  1.083645  20.066075169   FALSE FALSE
## Loan_Status        7.879631   0.003405937   FALSE FALSE
#There are no near-zero variance

Problem 2 - Distribution of Inputs

Visualize the distributions of the quantitative predictors (Term, NoEmp, DisbursementGross) with histograms (feel free to create a separate histogram for each variable) on the training set. Which variable(s) appear to be skewed?

ggplot(train_SBA, aes(x = NoEmp)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 30, fill = "lightgreen", color = "black", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "Histogram of Number of Employees", x = "NoEmp", y = "Count")

ggplot(train_SBA, aes(x = Term)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 30, fill = "lightgreen", color = "black", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "Histogram of Number of Employee Terms", x = "Term", y = "Count")

ggplot(train_SBA, aes(x = DisbursementGross)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 100, fill = "lightgreen", color = "black", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "Histogram of Disbursement Gross", x = "DisbursementGross", y = "Count")

Problem 3 - Box-Cox

Compute the \(\lambda\) values for all of the quantitative variables from problem 2 using the preProcess command on the training set. You might have to use the “add one” trick for a few of the variables. What values of \(\lambda\) did you get?

boxCox <- SBA_clean[, c("Term", "NoEmp", "DisbursementGross")]

boxCox <- boxCox + 1

pre <- preProcess(boxCox, method = "BoxCox")

pre$bc
## $Term
## Box-Cox Transformation
## 
## 73403 data points used to estimate Lambda
## 
## Input data summary:
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
##     1.0    61.0    85.0   110.3   121.0   512.0 
## 
## Largest/Smallest: 512 
## Sample Skewness: 0.967 
## 
## Estimated Lambda: 0.4 
## 
## 
## $NoEmp
## Box-Cox Transformation
## 
## 73403 data points used to estimate Lambda
## 
## Input data summary:
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
##    1.00    3.00    6.00   12.44   13.00  859.00 
## 
## Largest/Smallest: 859 
## Sample Skewness: 8.46 
## 
## Estimated Lambda: -0.3 
## 
## 
## $DisbursementGross
## Box-Cox Transformation
## 
## 73403 data points used to estimate Lambda
## 
## Input data summary:
##     Min.  1st Qu.   Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max. 
##     4001    50001   100001   195953   227001 11000001 
## 
## Largest/Smallest: 2750 
## Sample Skewness: 5.79 
## 
## Estimated Lambda: 0 
## With fudge factor, Lambda = 0 will be used for transformations

Problem 4 - Distribution of variables post-transform

Use predict to execute the transformations. Visualize the quantitative variables after transformation on the training set.

tData <- predict(pre,boxCox)

cdata <- tData %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = everything(), names_to = "Variable", values_to = "Value"
  )

ggplot(cdata, aes(x = Value)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "black", color = "black") +
  facet_wrap(~Variable, scales = "free") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Distributions After Box-Cox Transformation",
       x = "Transformed Value",
       y = "Count")

Problem 5 - Build/Test Model

Use the transformed training data as input to a logistic model.

Transform the test data. Don’t forget to transform the test data in the same way that you transformed the training data (using the preprocessing values from the training set).

Compare the performance (ROC area under the curve) on the transformed test set to our baseline (model5).

trainQuant <- train_SBA[, c("Term","NoEmp","DisbursementGross")]

testQuant <- test_SBA[,c("Term","NoEmp","DisbursementGross")]

trainQuant <- trainQuant + 1

testQuant <- testQuant +1

train_t <- predict(pre, trainQuant)
test_t  <- predict(pre, testQuant)

train_new <- train_SBA
test_new  <- test_SBA

train_new[, c("Term", "NoEmp", "DisbursementGross")] <- train_t
test_new[,  c("Term", "NoEmp", "DisbursementGross")] <- test_t

model_base <- glm(Loan_Status ~ .,
                  data = train_SBA,
                  family = binomial)

model2 <- glm(Loan_Status ~ .,
                data = train_new,
                family = binomial)

prob2 <- predict(model2,
                   newdata = test_new,
                   type = "response")

prob_base <- predict(model_base,
                     newdata = test_SBA,
                     type = "response")

roc_base <- roc(test_SBA$Loan_Status, prob_base)
## Setting levels: control = Paid_in_Full, case = Default
## Setting direction: controls < cases
auc_base <- auc(roc_base)

roc_bc <- roc(test_new$Loan_Status, prob2)
## Setting levels: control = Paid_in_Full, case = Default
## Setting direction: controls < cases
auc_bc <- auc(roc_bc)

auc_bc
## Area under the curve: 0.8023
auc_bc - auc_base
## [1] 0.004827803
#The transformation model improved by 0.0048 from 0.7975 to 0.8023

Problem 6 - Nonlinear terms

Experiment with some additional nonlinear terms for some of the quantitative variables (DisbursementGross, NoEmp, Term). Do they improve the ROC area under the curve?

model3 <- glm(
 Loan_Status ~ . +
    I(Term^2),
  data = train_new,
  family = binomial
)

probNl <- predict(model3,
                   newdata = test_new,
                   type = "response")

rocNl <- roc(test_new$Loan_Status, probNl)
## Setting levels: control = Paid_in_Full, case = Default
## Setting direction: controls < cases
aucNl <- auc(rocNl)

aucNl
## Area under the curve: 0.8034
model4 <- glm(
 Loan_Status ~ . +
    I(NoEmp^2),
  data = train_new,
  family = binomial
)

probNl2 <- predict(model4,
                   newdata = test_new,
                   type = "response")

rocNl2 <- roc(test_new$Loan_Status, probNl2)
## Setting levels: control = Paid_in_Full, case = Default
## Setting direction: controls < cases
aucNl2 <- auc(rocNl2)

aucNl2
## Area under the curve: 0.8051
#There is small consistant improvement.
